claimed_contribution: The paper explicitly claims to show when and why a store brand can become a source of store loyalty and higher retailer profitability, even without a store-brand cost or margin advantage over national brands.
It explicitly claims that this loyalty mechanism applies to sufficiently high-quality store brands, but not to low-cost, low-quality private labels.
It explicitly claims complementary roles of store and national brands, and provides empirical support using European chain evidence and U.S./Canadian household scanner data.
context: The paper studies grocery retailing in low-involvement packaged goods categories where competing stores carry national brands and may introduce store brands. Empirical support is drawn from chain-level evidence in the United Kingdom and France and household-level scanner data from the United States and Canada.
design_and_data: The authors develop a two-period game-theoretic model of two competing retailers on a Hotelling line, with consumer heterogeneity in brand-switching inertia and retailer decisions on advertising and pricing national versus store brands under rational expectations. The model assumes store-brand introduction in Period 1 and examines equilibrium outcomes when the store brand has no cost advantage relative to the national brand after quality adjustment. For evidence, they present retailer-level comparisons of private-label penetration, loyalty, and profitability for major UK and French chains. They also run household-level regressions using U.S. scanner panel data (494 households, 104 weeks, 22 categories, four supermarkets) and Canadian household panel data (685 households, two years, more than 85 categories).
main_findings: In the model, a sufficiently high-quality store brand can increase retailer total profits by creating store differentiation and loyalty through inertia, even when it has no cost advantage over the national brand.

The loyalty-profit mechanism does not hold for low-quality 'cheap and nasty' private labels; those strategies reinforce price competition, and with zero brand inertia store-brand introduction does not improve profits.
Empirical analyses show a positive association between store-brand penetration and store loyalty/profitability measures in UK/French chain data and in U.S./Canadian household-level data.
one_line_summary: The paper shows theoretically and supports empirically that high-quality store brands can build store loyalty and raise retailer profitability via consumer inertia, even without superior store-brand margins.
relation_to_prior_work: challenges the view that private-label value for retailers is mainly from higher margins and stronger bargaining power with manufacturers by introducing a loyalty-based mechanism tied to brand inertia and store differentiation. It also reconciles that literature with evidence that some quality store brands are not directly more profitable than national brands.
research_gap: Prior work and managerial explanations mostly attributed private-label gains to higher store-brand margins and improved bargaining leverage over manufacturers. The paper identifies an unresolved issue: retailers still carry quality store brands even when national brands can be more profitable on direct contribution metrics.
research_question: Why do retailers carry quality store brands when those brands may not have a cost or margin advantage over national brands? The paper asks whether, and under what conditions, store brands can create store differentiation, increase store loyalty, and improve retailer profitability.
